long-title: Health information is too hard to read and has too much jargon

content: People who run the systems
Health organisations
local authorities
Staff at every level
The general public
people who find reading tricky
People who find counting tricky

sections.long-title: What are the causes of the problem?
content: Systems that do not talk to each other
Organisations don't know there is a problem
Staff in organisations don't know there is a problem
Organisations don't have targets
Low staff skills in health literacy
Lack of staff time to rewrite materials
Lack of resources to rewrite materials
Not currently part of the culture of organisations
Organisation systems not fully accessible to staff
AI
Online and automated tools to help rewrite documents
Standardised systems
staff training
Good organisation policies
Better data systems to safely store information
Sharing resources
Using pictures, diagrams, videos and animations
standardised information

sections.long-title: What actions are involved in doing something about this?
content: Working with people receiving services
Developing automated systems
making the IT systems easier to use
Rewriting materials so that they are easy to read
Developing regional templates and systems
improving systems to include health literacy
giving staff training in health literacy
Making sure staff are trained often enough
training clinical staff
working with graphic illustrators, film makers and animation makers
Checking how complex materials are now (an audit)

sections.long-title: How will we deliver these actions?
content: Develop IT tools to aid simplifying health information
Automated systems
applying updates across the system
Authorised training including online and face to face
system improvements
developing online materials
work with people who can develop video and audio alternatives
always explain codes and jargon used in materials

sections.long-title: What do we expect to come out of these actions?
content: Clear language used across systems
health information that is easier to read and use
Health information available in other languages
health information in different formats
Less anxiety for people reading and using information

sections.long-title: How will we know things have changed?
content: Increased patient satisfaction
Lower readability levels of documents
Asking patients and the public what they think
Describing what patients and the public think
Higher number of languages in which health information is available
Higher quality of life scores
Higher number and percentage of materials written in clear language
Self-assessment questionnaires filled in by patients, members of the public and staff

sections.long-title: What short term changes will we see?
content: We will be developing action plans
Systems will work better for people
Increased satisfaction with services
Staff will be happier and more engaged
People will have better skills for health
Systems will be easier to navigate
Better information for health
Materials will be easier to use

sections.long-title: What do we think will happen in the longer term (3 years)? For example, increased health literacy, better disease control, improved health
content: Patients happier with services
Better health
Fewer people missing appointments
Materials that are easier to use
Reduced health inequalities
Better use of GP and hospital appointments
Less stress and depression
Improved health literacy of the public
More people completing treatment

sections.long-title: What are the current activities in this area?
